national_expectations.summary: DR Congo enter the Round of 32 having already exceeded every expectation: this is the Leopards' first World Cup knockout appearance in the nation's history, achieved on their return to the finals 52 years after competing as Zaire in 1974. Reaching the last 32 as the best-ranked of the third-placed teams (top points and the only positive goal difference among them) was framed by media as a historic milestone rather than a stepping stone, so anything further is treated as bonus. Coverage casts the Leopards as heavy underdogs for the next round but credits them with enough recent evidence (the AFCON runs and the Uzbekistan comeback) to be taken seriously rather than dismissed as a happy-to-be-here minnow.

national_mood.summary: The mood around the Leopards has been euphoric and emotionally charged since qualification. When the squad returned to Kinshasa after sealing their place, a normally short drive from the airport stretched into a near four-hour crawl as tens of thousands of fans in the national blue, yellow and red flooded the streets, waving flags, beating drums and blowing vuvuzelas in a hero's welcome. President Felix Tshisekedi staged an official reception and rewarded the players, lending the achievement the weight of a national event. Fans and the captain repeatedly tied the team's success to the country's wider hardships and an ongoing conflict, framing the run as a source of unity and pride for a nation that has endured difficult realities. After the Uzbekistan comeback, that pride deepened into belief. Pre-match mood specific to the England tie is thinly reported, so this section leans partly on the April homecoming coverage.

manager_situation.summary: Frenchman Sebastien Desabre is firmly established and widely credited as the architect of this golden era for the Leopards. The Uzbekistan win was another major tournament milestone in charge, following his record of guiding DR Congo to fourth place at the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations and the round of 16 at the 2025 edition. Reporters singled out his in-game management against Uzbekistan: trailing early, he abandoned the more defensive shape used in the first two group games for a bolder, more attacking setup with extra forwards, a change credited with overwhelming Uzbekistan in the second half and turning the match. His standing is secure and his tactical adaptability has been a central theme of the team's knockout breakthrough.

historical_context.summary: The 2026 finals marked DR Congo's return to the World Cup after a 52-year absence, their only previous appearance coming in 1974 under the name Zaire. Reaching the knockout stage is the first time the nation has ever advanced beyond the group phase at a World Cup. Their qualification path included victories over African heavyweights Cameroon and Nigeria and an intercontinental playoff win over Jamaica in March 2026, which ended the long wait. In the group stage they drew with Portugal, lost narrowly to Colombia and beat Uzbekistan 3-1, finishing third on four points with a plus-one goal difference, the strongest record among the third-placed qualifiers.

dominant_storylines.storyline: A first-ever knockout breakthrough and the redemption of a 52-year wait: media frame DR Congo's run as the most significant moment in the nation's football history, their first World Cup since competing as Zaire in 1974 and their first time ever escaping the group stage.

The Wissa-led comeback: trailing early to Uzbekistan, the Leopards roared back through Yoane Wissa's penalty, Fiston Mayele's strike and a late second from Wissa, a resilience-defining 3-1 win that supplied a new national memory and the points needed to advance.

Football as national catharsis: the homecoming celebrations, presidential rewards of cars and houses, and the captain's words tying the run to an unfinished war cast the Leopards as a unifying symbol of pride for a country grappling with hardship and conflict.

Underdog with credentials: the Leopards advance as heavy outsiders into a tie against a Group L winner, but their first-ever knockout run, Desabre's AFCON pedigree and the Uzbekistan comeback are cited as evidence they are not a ceremonial minnow.

attributed_quotes.quote: We're still here; the war isn't over yet. But first of all, I want to say thank you, because it's been 52 years.
attributed_quotes.context: Addressing supporters at the homecoming reception after the Leopards sealed World Cup qualification, tying the milestone to the country's 52-year wait and ongoing conflict.

attributed_quotes.quote: Every Leopard player here has already been given a car and a house by the government.
attributed_quotes.context: At the official state reception honoring the squad for qualifying, fulfilling a promised reward to the players.

local_availability_reporting.note: No credible reporting found on any DR Congo player being doubtful, injured or suspended for the Round of 32 as of 2026-06-28. Availability picture is unreported rather than confirmed fit.

gaps: DB FACTS list the Round of 32 opponent as TBD, but multiple credible outlets (ESPN, Yahoo Sports, Sports Mole, England Football, Al Jazeera) report the opponent is England (Group L winners), 1 July at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta. Per instructions next_opponent_code is kept TBD; this discrepancy is flagged for review.

DK Network (DraftKings) was removed as a source throughout because it is a betting/odds sportsbook network, excluded per the sourcing standard. The 'underdog with credentials' storyline was reworked to rest only on supported, non-betting framing (heavy outsiders vs a Group L winner) plus Desabre's AFCON pedigree.

FOX Sports does not state Desabre's specific AFCON record (2023 fourth place, 2025 round of 16); that fact is carried by 101 Great Goals. FOX was kept only for the claims it directly supports (tactical switch, 1974/Zaire, qualifying wins, Wissa/Mayele).

Some outlets (France24, Footmercato) loosely label the next round the 'Round of 16'/'huitiemes de finale'; the 48-team format's first knockout round is the Round of 32, consistent with the DB FACTS. Terminology inconsistency noted.
Could not retrieve an official FIFA/CAF/FECOFA source with extractable verbatim text, so no 'official'/fact_grade tier source was secured; all claims rest on press (discovery) tier.

No verbatim post-Uzbekistan quote from coach Desabre or Yoane Wissa could be locked with certainty (re-fetches returned no clean attributable quote), so those were excluded from attributed_quotes to avoid fabrication.
No specific, sourced DR Congo player injury, suspension or fitness doubt was found for the Round of 32 as of 2026-06-28; availability is unreported rather than confirmed.
No credible local-outlet reporting (e.g. Congolese press) on tactical plans or lineup intentions for the next match was located beyond general underdog framing.

Pre-match mood reporting specific to the upcoming knockout tie (as opposed to qualification-night euphoria) is thin; national_mood leans partly on the April homecoming coverage, which is noted in-section.
